name: Xu Zhu
title: Guan Yu
sub: Lord of the Magnificent Beard/Lord of the Beautiful Beard

desc.content1:
The legend of Guan Yu and his strong sense of chivalry and brotherhood has transcended two millennia and is still widely revered today in Chinese culture. Mention his deity name "Guan Gong" and most elder Chinese would relate to you legendary stories of Guan Yu's strong sense fairness, justice and brotherhood. The 2nd eldest among the three sworn brothers, Liu Bei, Guan Yu and Zhang Fei, Guan Yu is usually the most rational.

Guan Yu was a distinguished military commander of the Kingdom of Shu during the era of the Three Kingdoms. He was a steadfast companion to Liu Bei during their early campaigns and rose to prominence after overcoming the Yellow Turban Rebellion. Despite being captured during Cao Cao's Eastern Expedition, Guan Yu was held in high regard and treated with utmost respect, notably, Cao Cao had strong intentions of recruiting a warrior with such high caliber.
desc.content2: Like most of Shu's "combat" generals in WTK game, there is only a superficial link between their ability and the story. In the case of Guan Yu, the game makers decided to make it easy for Guan Yu to attack other players. Unlike Zhao Yun who has an advantage in dodging, Guan Yu's advantage only lies in attack. Perhaps it is a way of paying tribute to Guan Yu's courage and raw power.
desc.content3:
In a display of gratitude toward Cao Cao's kindness, he valiantly defeated the enemy general Yan Liang amidst overwhelming odds, leading to his prestigious appointment as the Marquis of Hanshou Village. Upon learning of Liu Bei's whereabouts, Guan Yu made a firm and unwavering choice to depart from Cao Cao's faction to reunite with Liu Bei and offer his loyal service once again.

Despite all the battles won by Guan Yu, people remember him best for his role in letting Cao Cao escape. When nearly all hope was lost for Cao Cao after the huge loss at Battle of Red Cliffs, he had the ill-fortune to be confronted by Guan Yu. Cao Cao knelt down before Guan Yu and said it was his fortune to die in the hands of the respected Guan Yu, rather than in the hands of some other unknown character. However Guan Yu let Cao Cao go because he was once indebted to Cao Cao, who spared his life in a reversal of fortunes many years before. The key thing to remember here is that Guan Yu is liable for punishment by death for this chivalrous act, yet he still let Cao Cao go. Now that is a classic example of chivalry indeed.

Guan Yu was captured by troops from the Kingdom of Wu after his own men deserted him. Guan Yu had severely underestimated his enemies and left the city of Jingzhou poorly guarded. While he was away attacking Cao Ren at Fancheng, the troops of Lv Meng moved in and captured Jingzhou. Guan Yu's men, who were mostly from Jing Zhou, deserted him to return to the safety of their family. Guan Yu was then captured at Maicheng. 

Guan Yu's exceptional courage and skill in governing and protecting Jingzhou brought him widespread acclaim. Yet, the dissolution of the Sun-Liu alliance and the betrayal by Sun Quan forced Guan Yu to withdraw to Maicheng, where he tragically fell into an ambush orchestrated by Sun Quan's troops, resulting in his capture and eventual execution. Admired for his unwavering commitment to righteousness, loyalty, and courage, Guan Yu is revered as the "God of War" in Chinese cultural traditions. He was executed together with his son Guan Ping because they refused to surrender.
desc.content4: His combat ability is incredible and his weapon the "Green Dragon Blade" has become the spear of legends.

desc.content1: In the old WTK, we used to say that Yuan Shao had heritage, and Liu Bei was a descendant of Han Emperors. That's nothing when you come face to face with Kong Rong... or so thinks the man. He is the descendant of Confucius himself. Never mind that Confucius died 700 years before he was born. Never mind that he is the 20th generation descendant and is probably further from Confucius than any of us are from Obama. He is a descendant!! Kong Rong himself was a famous Confucian scholar. He was a child prodigy in his childhood and a mediocre warlord in his adult hood. He later humiliated the “antagonist” Cao Cao several times, which eventually led to the execution of his whole family. That's not really the brightest thing to do, is it? Descendant of Confucius? PROVE IT!
desc.content2:
"Celebrity" refers to Kong Rong’s fame as a Confucian scholar. People with hidden ambition (hidden character card) cannot harm him. This ability is pretty powerful in the early stages, but useless in latter stages. This suits the fact that Kong Rong remains unscathed in his early days for the fear of losing support from Confucian scholars. 

In his later days, his fame could not protect him. "Comity" refers to his personality towards those who were admired by him. A famous story was “Kong Rong ceding the pear”, where the young Kong Rong gave the big pears for his brothers, and picked the smallest for himself, symbolizing filial love. As an official, he recommended Mi Heng to Cao Cao, saying that Mi Heng possessed talents superior to him. 

In Kingdom Wars, he could give his discarded cards (actively and passively) to other players.

desc.content1: Scrawny and wind-worn with age. This is the constant stereotypical image of Chinese military advisers, and these two aging old men fit that stereotype perfectly... well, sort of. Though the images and portraits show Zhang Zhao and Zhang Hong as old geezers, the pair were already advisers to the Wu kingdom since they were much younger. Collectively known as "The Two Zhang's", Zhang Zhao and Zhang Hong were first recruited by the warlord, Sun Jian, who was Sun Quan's father. The Two Zhang's were approximately the same age as Sun Jian and will stay loyal to the Wu Kingdom for decades till their death. Tragically, the two Zhang's went through the deaths of both Sun Jian, and his elder son, Sun Ce. It was on Sun Ce's deathbed that he famously said to his little brother Sun Quan, "Should [you] have doubts upon internal affairs, [you] must turn to Zhang Zhao; for outer matters [you] must consult Zhou Yu." Sun Quan wisely employed the Two Zhang's for the entire duration of his reign, and saw the Wu Kingdom rise to become one of the three crucial kingdoms of the era.
desc.content2:
"Blunt advice" refers to Zhang Zhao’s various advices to Sun Quan. As an official of the Wu Kingdom, Zhang Zhao aided the young Sun Quan to administrate internal affairs while Zhou Yu managed external affairs. Wise but stubborn, Zhang Zhao criticized Sun Quan whenever he found the Sun Quan's decision to be incorrect. Although Zhang Zhao was often correct, his blunt attitude angered Sun Quan to the extent that Sun Quan once thought of killing him. Thankfully Sun Quan was able to cool down and continued seeking his advice, which always benefited the Wu Kingdom.

"Stable Governance/Stabilization" refers to Zhang Hong’s contribution to Wu Kingdom. Firstly, Zhang Hong made a plan for Sun Ce, advising him to defeat the warlords Yuan Shu and Liu Biao to obtain land for Wu Kingdom's survival. Secondly, after the death of Sun Ce, Cao Cao intended to eliminate the Wu kingdom. It was Zhang Hong who utilized his diplomatic skills to stop Cao Cao, buying time for Sun Quan to cope with the internal issues of the state. Thirdly, Zhang Hong aided Sun Quan to consolidate his control in politics and in the military, even personally stopping Sun Quan’s reckless actions on the battlefield. Fourthly, he advised Sun Quan to set the capital of Wu Kingdom at Moling (present day Nanjing), a strategic location which is most suitable to be held tightly.
desc.content3:
Most famous achievement - Administration of Internal affairs of Wu Kingdom. There were many brilliant generals in Wu Kingdom, but few ministers existed. Sun Quan was too young to handle the internal affairs of the whole kingdom, and his impulsive actions jeopardized the safety of his realm. It was the two Zhangs who corrected his mistake and stabilized politics, allowing Wu Kingdom to prosper.

Cause of death - Natural causes. The two Zhangs died as old men, still faithful and loyal to the Sun family as they have been throughout their entire lives.

desc.content1:
You know how some people's lives serve as a reminder to others not to make that same mistake? Tian Feng is one of those unfortunate people. Undoubtedly a brilliant advisor to Yuan Shao, Tian Feng presented many astute plans to his lord. Even Cao Cao praised Tian Feng for his abilities. Xun Yu went so fa as to call Tian Feng "Bold but treacherous".

But why did I say that Tian Feng's life is a lesson for us? Tian Feng's biggest mistake was to choose the wrong leader to follow. Yuan Shao was an indecisive leader and he rarely knew which advice was good, and which was plain suicide. Yuan shao chose not to listen to Tian Feng, and even imprisoned him. The foolish Yuan Shao eventually led his army to defeat in the Battle of Guan du. During the retreat of Yuan Shao, Pang Ji accused Tian Feng of “looking happy to hear the defeat of Yuan Shao”. Yuan Shao was enraged and sentenced Tian Feng to death. Tian Feng predicted this, and chose to kill himself before the execution. Bottom line... choose your boss wisely. Learn from Tian Feng and don't let his death go in vain!
desc.content2: “Death Remonstrance” refers to Tian Feng’s upright actions to admonish his ruler. This ability can be useful against Wei characters (that values cards over health), but to use it is to stake your life, as you need to have no on-hand cards left. “With the Tide” refers to his tragic fate. During his imprisonment, Tian Feng predicted that Yuan Shao would pardon him if the battle was victorious, and kill him if defeated. His prediction was right, as the defeated Yuan Shao sentenced him to death. This enforced ability allows Tian Feng to gain power (cards) if his allies win the battle (forcing someone to the brink of death), and loses health if his allies are defeated.

desc.content1: His description portrays him as a mass-murderer, and history seems to back that up. After the death of Dong Zhuo, Jia Xu adviced Li Jue to take advantage of the chaos in Chang’an and grab the Imperial power for himself. This would prove to be the biggest black mark on Jia Xu's life as Li Jue and Guo Si went on a rampage in Chang An, killing and abducting innocent civilians, burning and robbing property. Later historians place the blame squarely on Jia Xu for the holocaust. The fact is Jia Xu is an incredibly gifted advisor whose predictions have come true many times. The sad thing is his leaders did not always listen to him! While serving under Cao Cao, Jia Xu was one of the rare few who did not support the attack on Wu. Not heeding this advice, Cao Cao later met with disaster at Red Cliffs. Years later while serving under Cao Pi, Jia Xu again advised a defensive strategy instead of supporting Cao Pi's attack on either Wu or Shu. Once again, his advice proved to be correct as Cao Pi was not victorious.
desc.content2:
No direct link is made to "Perfect Murder", though it can be inferred that we want to add drama to this "cold-blooded murderer". "Turmoil" is referring to the massacre at Chang An carried out by Li Jue and Guo Si.

"Iron Curtain" is interesting. Jia Xu happens to be the longest-living advisor in the Three Kingdoms Era, and the reason for this is rather unglamorous. He is remembered as someone who will save his own ass before the higher purpose. Numerous times he has switched allegiance as his leaders started to fail, finally ending up at Wei till his death. Even then, Jia Xu stayed away from expressing emotions that would compromise his true thoughts. In his later years he became a recluse, and did not even speak in private to anyone else. The "Curtain" in this case is his silence and evasive character, making him less susceptible to ploys and schemes. Thus the ability is apt for him since it makes him impervious to a large number of tool cards.
desc.content3: Advising Li Jue to Guo Si to take the empire for themselves. Eventually Lu Jue and Guo Si tore each other apart and Jia Xu prudently distanced himself from them.

desc.content1:
He is a brand new character specially created for Kingdom Wars. We all know that Ma Chao was one of Liu Bei's most trusted generals but his father, Ma Teng, was quite exemplary as well. 

Born into poverty, he crawled from rags to governorship through his charisma and skills. Alas, Ma Teng was not single-minded in his loyalty, switching back and forth between ally and traitor. His reckoning came when he plotted against Cao Cao, only for the plot to be discovered. Ma Teng died and nearly lost his entire family. Ma Chao escaped though, and the rest is history!
desc.content2: “Horsemanship” is the traditional ability of generals from Liang Province, for leading elite forces of cavalry. “Call of Valour” was a term originally used to describe the charsimatic look of Ma Teng, and refers to Ma Teng’s superb leadership in leading his troops. This ability provides a burst of cards for all players of your Kingdom, symbolizing the power of Ma Teng’s massive troops of horsemen that ravaged the enemy in a sudden.

name: Zhen Ji
title: Zhuge Liang
sub: Sleeping Dragon/Hidden Dragon/Prime Minister in Languish/The Prime Minister who is Past his Prime
weapon1: Pang Tong ( Younger ) / Jiang Wei ( Elder ) /  Huang Yueying ( both Younger & Elder)

desc.content1: Zhuge Liang, held the position of prime minister in the Shu Han dynasty during the Three Kingdoms period. He was renowned for his remarkable talents as a statesman, strategist, innovator, and literacy figure. During his formative years, Zhuge Liang accompanied his uncle Zhuge Xuan to Jingzhou, where he resided in seclusion in Longzhong. It was during this time that Liu Bei made a total of three visits to persuade Zhuge Liang to join him as a military strategist. Upon accepting Liu Bei's offer, Zhuge Liang devised the "Longzhong Plan," a strategic blueprint aimed at assisting Liu Bei in the establishment of Shu. Zhuge Liang was celebrated for his expertise in I Ching divination. Additionally, his keen observation of the celestial phenomena endowed him with the ability to forecast important events with exceptional precision. Zhuge Liang exemplified diligence and caution in his approach, strictly adhering to principles of rewarding merits and punishing wrongdoings. During his lifetime, he led five military campaigns to the Central Plains in the north. During one of these expeditions, Zhuge Liang famously deployed the "Empty Fortress Strategem" to outwit and deter Sima Yi's advancing army. Zhuge Liang passed away at the age of 54 on the Wuzhang Plains, succumbing to an illness that was exacerbated by his unyielding workload and stress. Zhuge Liang's steadfast dedication and exhibit the ideas of loyalty and wisdom which are now deeply rooted in traditional Chinese culture.
desc.content2:
Zhuge Liang actively trained the troops of Shu during lull periods. His mastery of military formations gained the respect of many of his adversaries, not least Sima Yi in the later years. The Eight Trigrams Formation is one of the most famous that Zhu Ge Liang used very well.

Arson was used by him from the very first battle he oversaw, which resulted in huge losses for the enemy. Later, both him and Zhou Yu came up with the same idea to use fire against Cao Cao at the battle at Red Cliffs.

Of course, Zhu Ge Liang was depicted as so god-damned smart that every teeny weeny little action of his enemies, he understood their intent. His "See Through" plays on the fact that no tool or ploy works on him, since he can see through it all instantly. Smart.

In Romance of the Three Kingdoms, Zhuge Liang is given almost god-like powers that can predict the changes in weather, accurate to within the day that wind directions change. He is able to make these predictions from his knowledge of reading the stars, thus the game ability to "predict" what cards are coming up next with his "Stargazing". Ok so its outright manipulation of the cards coming up next, but heck might as well dramatize it more!

The "Empty Fortress" comes from the famous ploy he uses to fool Sima Yi. As all his troops have been sent out in missions, he was unable to defend himself from Sima Yi's attack of his current location Xicheng. In classical reverse psychology , Zhuge Liang empties the fortress, opens the city gates wide to welcome the invaders, and calmly plays the zither atop the gate in full view of the enemy. Sima Yi suspects an ambush and thus retreats. To replicate that effect, we gave the player using Elder Zhuge Liang insusceptibility to ATTACK and DUEL when the player has no more cards left to defend himself or herself.

During a ritual ceremony to extend his life (no kidding!), Wei Yan barges in and disturbs Zhuge Liang. He dies soon after from the effects of the disturbance.
desc.content3:
Most famous achievement - In his younger days, before joining Liu Bei, Zhuge Liang was sort of a guru in his hometown. The story depicts the countryside where Zhuge Liang lived to be similar to Barney-the-purple-dinosaur land; everyone smiling and singing happily. Though no direct link is made between their state-of-mind and Zhuge Liang's presence there, nonetheless it makes for more interesting story-telling to believe that was the case. He would have chosen to stay as a happy farmer for the rest of his life had Liu Bei not paid him repeated visits.

Borrowing arrows with straw boats? There really isn't ONE famous achievement because he has so many!
desc.content4: Though the feathered fan is Zhuge Liang's prized item in the story, the weapon in this game is not a perfect match for his abilities. Feathered Fan brings the same advantage to any other player anyways. Besides, with half the entire deck being of red-suited cards, Younger Zhuge Liang has more than enough fire attribute offensives as it is!

desc.content1: 7.	Huang Yueying, the wife of the Prime Minister Zhuge Liang of the Shu Han kingdom during the turbulent Three Kingdoms period, was born into the prestigious Huang family as the daughter of the renowned scholar Huang Chengyan from Miannan. She was an invaluable and reliable partner to Zhuge Liang, possessing exceptional intelligence and knowledge. Huang Yueying is often portrayed as a master mechanic and inventor. While some may have questioned Huang Yueying's beauty in light of her exceptional intelligence, those are unfounded misconceptions. Huang Yueying's brilliance played a crucial role in supporting Zhuge Liang during his military campaigns and strategic endeavors. Her ingenuity is highly praised in Chinese literature and popular culture.
desc.content2:
In WTK games, Huang Yueying is one of the most popular characters to use. She can be deadly when luck runs in her favor, and the odds usually side her since all she needs are Spell/ Stratagem cards.

Both of her skills are based on her superior intellect. Exactly how smart is she?
desc.content3: Most famous achievement - Getting herself married to Zhuge Liang?! Since the story goes that she is ugly as heck that is quite an accomplishment don't you think? As mentioned, Huang Yueying is usually remembered for hiding her face behind veils to conceal her hideousness to potential suitors.

desc.content1: The ruffian. The impulsive. The brute. Zhang Fei is the youngest amongst the 3 sworn brothers. Compared with his 2 elder sworn brothers, Liu Bei and Guan Yu, Zhang Fei tends to get in trouble for his quick temper and his love for alcohol. Some say his fighting ability is the strongest of the three. Known for his formidable prowess as a warrior in battle, Zhang Fei is also acknowledged as a "One Man Army," a title he shares with his sworn brother Guan Yu. In historical and literary accounts, Zhang Fei is fondly remembered as a courageous and loyal warrior.
desc.content2: Zhang Fei is famous for his loud roar. His “Bellow/Battle Cry” that scared away an entire Wei army. That, however, has nothing to do with the general's skill to repeatedly use ATTACK. You kind of get the feeling that the game makers based the skill of Shu's generals not so much on their historical basis, but more for the completeness of the game mechanism (i.e.: "it would be nice if a player could do THIS... let's allow Zhang Fei to do that").
desc.content3:
Ask any Chinese in the know what is Zhang Fei's most awesome achievement and they would undoubtedly say "scaring the shit out of Cao Cao's army at the bridge of Changbanqiao". He roared so loud that one enemy general fell off his horse and died while 10,000 enemy troops ran away in fear.

Zhang Fei's brutish and unreasonable nature makes him a terrible superior to work with. After Guan Yu died, Zhang Fei ordered Zhang Da and Fan Jiang  to prepare mourning clothes for his entire army within 3 days. Both of them pleaded that it was impossible to accomplish. Zhang Fei put down an ultimatum: that they would be beheaded if it was not done in 3 days. Fearing for their lives, Zhang Da and Fan Jiang cut off Zhang Fei's head while he was asleep and surrendered themselves to Wu Kingdom.
desc.content4: This weapon is a wonderful match for Zhang Fei. The most irritating thing that can happen to a strong attacking player like Zhang Fei is to have no ATTACK cards on-hand to use. Serpent Spear allows him to use ATTACK by discarding any 2 cards. The flip side is Zhang Fei rarely has the opportunity to accumulate more than 6 on-hand cards. Still, sometimes all it takes to end the game (and save the world) is to be able to ATTACK twice consecutively.

desc.content1: If there was a commando spirit in any of the Three Kingdoms generals, it would be Zhou Tai. He began his military career as a pirate-convert under Sun Ce, but Sun Quan quickly took a liking and requested Zhou Tai be transferred over to his command. It was from then on that Zhou Tai would save Sun Quan many times from certain death. In one significant scenario in the story, Sun Quan asked Zhou Tai to remove his clothing during a banquet and show his battle scars for all those present. Zhou Tai remembers the battle behind every scar, most of which he earned valiantly serving the cause of the Sun Quan's Wu Kingdom. It is thus that Sun Quan treats Zhou Tai as one of his most trusted and capable generals, just like a brother to him.
desc.content2: His ability "Undying" is a wonderful match from the story! Many times Zhou Tai risked life and limb to save Sun Quan, each time getting injured and cut up but he survives! This ability is a tribute to the Zhou Tai.
desc.content3:
Most famous achievement - Immense bravery in rescuing Sun Quan at the battle of Ruxukou. Upon the failure of Sun Quan's attack, he found himself surrounded by enemy troops. Zhou Tai had already dashed to safety, but seeing that Sun Quan was nowhere to be seen, dashed back into the chaos of battle once again. He told Sun Quan to follow behind as he slashed a path out to safety. Yet Sun Quan was once again surrounded very quickly and all seemed lost. Zhou Tai dashed into battle a second time, but this time the enemy was raining arrows on them. Zhou Tai told Sun Quan to dash straight out to safety as he covered to rear this time. They succeeded but Zhou Tai was injured in numerous places in his body. But that wasn't the end. After Sun Quan was safe, he dashed into the chaos a third time to rescue Xu Sheng successfully.

Cause of death - Zhou Tai died of illness at an old-age. It is estimated that he was 62 years old at the time of his death.

desc.content1:
Brave, ferocious, but not the quickest thinker. Ji Ling was the chief of Yuan Shu’s forces and wielded the Two-bladed Trident, one of the new weapons in Kingdom Wars. His skills were said to match those of Guan Yu, but in one famous encounter, Ji Ling was knee-deep in a clumsy predicament. While on orders to destroy Liu Bei, Ji Ling was summoned by Lv Bu to a meeting. What Ji Ling did not realize was that Lv Bu was planning to coax Ji Ling to back away from Liu Bei. Lv Bu told Ji Ling that if should he (Lv Bu) be able to strike his halberd with an arrow from150 paces away, Ji Ling would fold his troops and go home. Ji Ling claimed that such a feat was impossible, thus he foolishly gave his word. 

Cue one of the biggest face palms in Three Kingdom's history as Lv Bu comfortably let fly an arrow that struck his halberd with a loud clang. Ji Ling marched on home to a momentous scolding from his lord!
desc.content2:
There are four implicit meanings of “Double Edged”. Firstly, it refers to the bet between Ji Ling and Lv Bu. If his opponent wins the bet, Ji Ling would be forced to retreat. In terms of game mechanism, if he does not win the points duel, he would lose his chance of action. Secondly, it refers to his weapon, the Two-bladed Trident, which was three-pointed and two-edged. Thirdly, if Ji Ling wins the points duel, he would be able to strike twice (the virtual ATTACK and the ATTACK used in the action phase).

Fourthly, if Ji Ling loses the points duel, he would lose his action phase, so the ability is double-edged.

name: Xiahou Dun
title: Pang De
sub: Swepting Knight/Rider and Horse as One

desc.content1: The rider on a white horse, Pang De is a very capable military general that served under Ma Cha and his father, Ma Teng. After the death of Ma Teng, Pang De and Ma Chao resisted the attacks of Cao Cao but were unable to hold on to their city of Hanzhong. Ma Chao fled to join Liu Bei in Shu while Pang De surrendered to Wei. Cao Cao thought highly of Pang De and gave him a high position in the military. However at the battle of Fancheng, Pang De's loyalty came into question. This was because his old partner Ma Chao and his elder brother were aiding the enemy in that very siege. To prove his loyalty, Pang De kow-towed to Cao Cao till his head bled and brought along his own coffin into battle to prove that he would rather die than return defeated. Pang De did not succeed and was executed by Guan Yu.
desc.content2: Pang De has always been known for his superb horsemanship and is even nicknamed the White Horse General for his white steed. Therefore "Horse Riding" is no surprise.
desc.content3:
Most famous achievement - Carrying his coffin into battle. The coffin is intended to send a message to Cao Cao that either Guan Yu or himself will be in the coffin by the end of the battle. This establishes his determination and loyalty to Cao Wei. It is also awesome fodder for a dramatized story.

Pang De was eventually caught by Guan Yu when Fan Cheng became flooded due to the torrential rains. Pang De refused to kneel to Guan Yu or surrender. Guan Yu had no alternative but to execute him.

desc.content1: "Morrigan" from Darkstalkers. Get the picture? Unfortunately, Lady Zou cannot transform into bats or suck the life out of you. But she very nearly did bring about the death of Cao Cao with her provocative flirts. Like a story out of a soft-porn novel, the rendezvous of Lady Zou with Cao Cao was an adulterous episode of one man messing with his ally's wife. And how do adulterous couples fair in the end? Tragically, of course! The cuckoo-ed husband Zhang Xiu raided Cao Cao's army and very nearly killed him. Cao Cao escaped, but suffered the loss of his most beloved bodyguard, Dian Wei, and his eldest son Cao Ang. So you see, kids, adultery... adultery's bad... um-kay?
desc.content2: Both "Femme Fatale" and "Allure" related to Lady Zou's charm. Her beauty can force all hidden characters to stay "asleep", and their abilities cannot be activated within her turn.

name: Zhang Liao
title: Liu Bei
sub: Noble Hero Amidst the Chaos/Hero arising from Troubled Times

desc.content1: Liu Bei is the founding ruler of the Kingdom of Shu during the Three Kingdoms Era. The lack of material resources and social status never deterred his aspiration for greatness. In his early days, Liu Bei faced numerous obstacles and had to serve under various warlords. However, he remained steadfast in his belief in benevolence. After joining forces with Sun Quan in the pivotal Battle of Red Cliffs, Liu Bei secured a significant triumph over Cao Cao, laying the foundation for the establishment of the kingdom of Shu Han through his control over Jingzhou and Yizhou. Liu Bei was revered as a compassionate ruler with a keen eye for talent, entrusting his loyal subordinates with unwavering faith. His benevolent rule and moral uprightness deeply resonated with the people, attracting a devoted following of distinguished individuals such as Zhao Yun, arguably one of the mightiest warrior during that era. In the third year of Zhangwu (223 AD), Liu Bei passed away from illness in Baidicheng at 63. Recognizing his contributions and virtues, he was honored with the title Zhaolie after his death.
desc.content2: Liu Bei is regarded as the benevolent leader in Romance of the Three Kingdoms, usually generous and kind to his followers. The ability to give cards to any player mirrors Liu Bei's generosity.
desc.content3:
He built up the Kingdom of Shu despite humble beginnings as a straw-weaver.

Official records is due to dysentery, though the legend goes that he fell sick due to despair and regret for his massive loss at the Battle of Yiling . Prior to that battle, Liu Bei launched a massive attack on Wu against Zhuge Liang’s advice to avenge the death of his sworn brothers Guan Yu and Zhang Fei. Liu Bei was eventually defeated by Lu Xun and loses nearly all of Shu's generals.

desc.content1:
Where Wei had Deng Ai, Shu had Jiang Wei, Wu had Ding Feng! A general that ascended from very bottom, he first made his career as a captain under Gan Ning. He was usually assigned to unimportant missions along with Xu Sheng, such as chasing Zhuge Liang at the Battle of Red Cliffs. 

However, in the late years of Wu Kingdom, Ding Feng climbed up the ranks incredibly fast. At his peak, he went from being a soldier to a minister of Wu Kingdom. He achieved two important accomplishments. One of them was the Battle of Dongxing, in which Ding Feng resisted the invasion of Wei Kingdom, led by Sima Zhao. The second one was purging Sun Chen, a vile regent that created pandemonium in the Wu Kingdom. Alas, arrogance got the better of him and he was eventually exiled for his military failures in his later years.
desc.content2: Both “Close Combat” and “Swift and Deadly” came from the Battle of Dongxing. Learning the death of Sun Quan, Sima Zhao, the second son of Sima Yi and the de facto leader of Wei Kingdom, led a vast army to invade Wu Kingdom. In order to surprise raid the camp of the Wei force, Ding Feng ordered his subordinates to withdraw their heavy armor, rushing in the snow to defeat the Wei force using only short sabers. In the game, Ding Feng can withdraw his heavy arms (discard a card) to approach his enemies, devastating his enemies with “Close-quarter Combat”.

name: Cao Pi
title: Gongsun Zan
sub: The General on the White Stallion

desc.content1: Gongsun Zan, also known by his courtesy name Bogui, was a renowned general and warlord in the waning years of the Eastern Han dynasty. Born into a noble family, Gongsun Zan only assumed a minor official position due to his mother's lowly background. His striking appearance and exceptional talents endeared him to the chief of the prefecture, propelling his steady rise to the rank of Zhonglang General and cementing his authority in the border regions. Following a dispute with his superior Liu Yu, he committed the ultimate betrayal by killing Liu Yu and taking the court envoy hostage. Despite his previous dominance in the northern territories, his downfall came swiftly in a fateful clash with Yuan Shao. In a final act of desperation, he chose to set himself ablaze, concluding his life in a tragic yet legendary fashion.

Zhen Ji, who later became known as Empress Wenzhao, was born in the year 183 AD in Wuji County, Zhongshan. Zhen Ji, initially married to Yuan Xi, the second son of Yuan Shao, was taken into Cao Pi's custody after Cao Cao captured Yecheng. Subsequently, she became the wife of Emperor Wen of Wei, Cao Pi, and gave birth to Cao Rui and Princess Dongxiang. After Cao Pi's coronation, Zhen Ji voiced her resentment for falling out of favor. Enraged, Cao Pi commanded her to take her own life, and she was subsequently buried in Yecheng. Following her son Cao Rui's accession to the throne, Zhen Ji was honored as Empress Wenzhao after she passed on, and her tomb was relocated to the Chaoyang Mausoleum. Zhen Ji was celebrated for her exquisite beauty and remarkable talents, establishing herself as a renowned beauty of the Three Kingdoms era.

Pan Feng was a fictional character wielding a poleaxe and was a general serving the weak warlord Han Fu. In ROTK, Pan Feng was one many generals slayed by Hua Xiong, and he had a grand total of 2 lines of airtime in the entire story! 

So why put such a non-consequential character in Kingdom Wars? 
Apparently, Pan Feng was repeatedly poked fun of by the online population and sarcastically given the title "The most powerful general in the Three Kingdoms Era". From this sprang forth numerous parody biographies that exaggerate his abilities to ridiculous extents.
desc.content2:
The name of his ability relates to his weapon. 
Pan Feng and Pan Zhang who captured Guan Yu and took his crescent blade. By the way, this ability combines well with the Rock Cleaving Axe, the only axe weapon card in the game.
desc.content3: One of the most famous parodies of Pan Feng was “The Great Biography of the Unparalleled Pan Feng”, in which Pan Feng was a superior general of great strength and wisdom. He used a poleaxe of 180catties (Guan Yu's only weighed 82 catties), and served Han Fu after seven visits by Han Fu himself (a parody of Zhuge Liang serving Liu Bei after three personal visits by Liu Bei). He could beat up Yan Liang and Wen Chou easily. Fear of his presence, Yuan Shao conspired to kill him by giving him.

desc.content1: Hua Xiong, a distinguished military general who served under Dong Zhuo during the late Eastern Han dynasty, was highly acclaimed for his ferocity in battle. In the story of The Romance of the Three Kingdoms, he volunteered and led 50,000 infantry troops to stop the armies of incoming warlords and defeated many of them in a row. He was subsequently slain by Guan Yu, and contributed to Guan Yu’s legend of “Slaying Hua Xiong while the wine is still warm.” In the official historical records of the "Three Kingdoms," the birth year of Hua Xiong remains shrouded in mystery, he is briefly mentioned in Sun Jian's campaign against Dong Zhuo, where he ultimately met his demise at the hands of Sun Jian, a contrast against the story of The Romance of the Three Kingdoms.

Hua Tuo, a native of Qiao County in Pei State, known by the courtesy name Yuanhua, was a renowned Chinese physician who thrived during the waning years of the Eastern Han dynasty.

With a vast breadth of medical knowledge, Hua Tuo demonstrated exceptional skill in surgery. Revered as the "Magical Hands" and the "Pioneer of Surgical Techniques" by future generations, his healing abilities were unmatched.
desc.content2:
"First Aid" could have some connection with him saving Guan Yu from the poison arrow. Though Guan Yu wasn't exactly on his deathbed yet, the wound was life-threatening and Hua Tuo did save him.

"Anti-Plague/Prodigal Healer" on the other hand, is very much linked to the story. Hua Tuo had with him a book of his works known as the "Book of the Green Salve". This book contained numerous medical techniques of saving lives, but was destroyed when he died. Though not told in the story, the historical accounts mention that this book was burnt by Hua Tuo himself when the prison guard refused to accept it.
desc.content3: Throughout his lifetime, Hua Tuo practiced medicine in diverse locations, honing his skills instead of pursuing a formal career. He tragically met his end at the hands of the paranoid Cao Cao, who imprisoned and subjected him to torture until his eventual death. His medical expertise and ethical standards continue to be pursued worldwide.

desc.content1:
Best remembered as the martial-arts loving wife of Liu Bei, Sun Shangxiang is the younger sister of Sun Quan. Since young, Sun loved martial arts and made all her maids carry swords wherever they go. She was offered to Liu Bei as his third wife by Sun Quan superficially, it appears to be a strengthening of Wu and Shu alliance. In reality, the marriage offered was a trap plotted out by Zhou Yu to lure Liu Bei into the Kingdom of Wu and hold him ransom in return for the land of Jingzhou. Yet in one of the most lauded schemes of Zhuge Liang, Liu Bei not only successfully married Sun Shangxiang, he also escapes back to Shu-Han with her unscathed. In his rage, Zhou Yu orders his troops to kill Liu Bei even if it means having to kill Sun Shangxiang, but the chase met with an ambush from Zhuge Liang and Zhou Yu ends up losing both Sun Shangxiang and thousands of troops.

Thus the famous saying: "To lose a lady in addition to losing numerous troops". which means "to suffer a double whammy after trying to take advantage of something".
desc.content2:
"Diplomatic Marriage/ Betrothment" is, of course, referring to her marriage with Liu Bei. The inside joke is this ability is commonly scandalized into "Intercourse" and since it can be used with ANY male character, she has to be a slut of sorts. It does not help that her character art gave her a pair of huge jugs spilling out of her top.

"Lady Warrior/ Daredevil" refers to her love for martial arts, but little connection is made with the mechanics of the ability with the storyline.
desc.content3: Most famous achievement - Her "escape" back to Wu. In another ploy, Sun Shangxiang was tricked into believing her aging mother was very ill. At the time, Liu Bei was away conquering the Western regions near Chengdu. She decided to leave for Wu and bring Liu Bei's son, Liu Shan with her. This threw up a huge fracas as Zhao Yun and Zhang Fei braved enemy arrows to convince her to return. Alas, Sun Shangxiang would not listen, but relented in releasing Liu Shan. Upon finding out it was a ploy, Sun Shangxiang was extremely upset. She never got to see Liu Bei again.

desc.content1: Best known as the father of Sun Ce, Sun Quan and Sun Shangxiang, Sun Jian was one of the warlords who took part in the early coalition against Dong Zhuo. In fact, Dong Zhuo and Sun Jian were bitter enemies even before Dong Zhuo took control of the imperial family. One significant event in the life of Sun Jian was finding the imperial seal in a well in the palace ruins at Luoyang. Sun Jian wanted to keep the seal for himself and, thus, control huge power within his grasps. Unfortunately for him, he was betrayed and Yuan Shao, then the leader of the coalition, confronted Sun Jian. Sun Jian vehemently denied having the seal, but Yuan Shao was convinced he was lying. Yuan Shao sent Liu Biao to intercept Sun Jian, and it was under Liu Biao that Sun Jian's life met an abrupt end.
desc.content2: The name "Heroic Spirit" is to be taken metaphorically, it describes Sun Jian's contribution to destroying Dong Zhuo. Being the first in the coalition of 18 warlords to attack Dong Zhuo, Sun Jian almost lost his life to Hua Xiong, but his initial strike was enough to cower Dong Zhuo and dampen his army's morale. Even after Sun Jian's death, the Heroic Spirit displayed in his early attacks against Dong Zhuo served Dong Zhuo's enemies well. Thus the link to the ability with Sun Jian suffering damage first, yet able to leverage on that to assist his team mates or disconcert his opponents.

desc.content1: We all know a rebellious kid back in school who just refused to listen to instructions. Wei Yan is a competent Shu general who was similar to that kid. Though not an outright rebel, Wei Yan always had that streak in him that made him difficult to lead. If there was a phrase that he would say, it would probably go along the lines of "Why should I do that!?" In true dramatic fashion, Wei Yan became the "bad guy" when he revolted against Zhuge Liang and Shu. In today's world, he would probably make a pretty successful entrepreneur!
desc.content2: A vampirical character is fun to have in a game, though there is no mention of any blood-sucking characters in Romance of the Three Kingdoms. Of course, Wei Yan was never a vampire and wasn't even portrayed anywhere near one. So this ability is purely just for gaming sakes.
desc.content3:
Most famous achievement - The rescue of Huang Zhong from execution at the city of Changsha. Wei Yan got pissed off by Han Xuan's distrust of Huang Zhong and decided to take matters into his own hands. He killed Han Xuan, rescued Huang Zhong, then surrendered the city to Liu Bei. Huang Zhong went on to become one of Liu Bei's most important generals. If not for Wei Yan, there would be no legend of Huang Zhong.

Cause of death - After Zhuge Liang passed on, Wei Yan openly revolted against Shu-Han. Zhuge Liang, being all godly and smart, knew that Wei Yan was going to revolt once he died. Therefore he tipped off Wei Yan's partner Ma Dai, and laid a trap. In the midst of open revolt, Wei Yan challenged Shu-Han's forces and shouted "Who dares to kill me?”. Ma Dai happily decapitated him from behind.

desc.content1: One of "the Three", Sun Quan is the ruler who ultimately formed the Kingdom of Wu. He took over the reigns of ruler from his elder brother, Sun Ce, at the age of 18 and led the Wu Kingdom till his death. He is the longest serving ruler of the era. Compared to Sun Ce who was known as the "Little Conqueror", Sun Quan was not a strong combat fighter, but he was able to win the hearts and respect of his generals and people with his charisma. He was surrounded with loyal and capable talents, which he prudently retained. The Kingdoms of Wu and Shu formed an alliance to resist Cao Cao's attacks. However, Sun Quan's short alliance with Liu Bei became a sore point as the wits of Wu were no match for the prescient Zhuge Liang. Regardless, the Kingdom of Wu lasted longer than both Wei and Shu. It was the last to be defeated by the Sima family, which marked the end of the Three Kingdoms era and the beginning of the Jin Dynasty.
desc.content2: "Rebalancing/Equilibrium" refers to Sun Quan's decision to ally with Liu Bei so as to counter the immense army of Cao Cao. Before the battle of Red Cliffs, Cao Cao has already amassed an army far greater than either Shu or Wu. With the alliance, both the kingdoms were able to restore the balance of power and prevent a complete overrun by Cao Cao.
desc.content3: In 229, Sun Quan declared himself emperor and founded the Kingdom of Wu, which facilitated the economic growth of the Jiangnan region. In his later years, Sun Quan grappled with internal conflicts among potential successors. He passed away in the year 252 AD at the age of 71.

desc.content1: After the Kingdom of Shu had been established in the west for several years, Meng Huo invaded Shu from the south. By this time, Liu Bei had died and Zhuge Liang made all the major decisions as Prime Minister. Meng Huo sarcastically commented that the occupation of Shu Han over the western lands was a barbaric act, thus Meng Huo was going to claim back the land where their ancestors roamed. Zhu Ge Liang decided that an all-out war would exhaust supplies as well as cause too many innocent casualties. He also predicted that even if Shu Han was victorious, the barbarians would retaliate or harbor thoughts of revenge, thus it was best that Meng Huo and his people can be subdued by winning their respect. Meng Huo attacked Shu on different occasions but Zhuge Liang outsmarted him every single time, capturing Meng Huo but freeing him. Every time Meng Huo blamed external circumstances for his defeat and refused to acknowledge Zhuge Liang was the stronger. In the end, Zhuge Liang captured Meng Huo 7 times before Meng Huo finally gave in and subdued his people. From then on, the barbarians never invaded Shu Han again and were loyal to Zhuge Liang.
desc.content2: Meng Huo's abilities have direct links to the story. He is the King of the Barbarians, therefore it is no surprise that his is "the smoking gun" for any damage caused by BARBARIANS. "Arise" probably has links to being released by Zhuge Liang 7 times. Meng Huo had the "never say die" attitude that pushed him to attack Shu repeatedly, despite so many failed attempts.
desc.content3:
Repeatedly attacking Shu, but ultimately was not Zhuge Liang's match. Still, most would have given up after the 2nd or 3rd attempt. Meng Huo kept on fighting for 7 times.

 The story does not further mention Meng Huo after he admits defeat, however historical records state that Meng Huo might have become an official in Shu and he died of old-age. Word of caution though, the historical (real) Meng Huo deviates significantly from the story. He was never caught 7 times by Zhuge Liang, and he was not even a barbarian, much less the barbarian king.

desc.content1: The only female in the entire Romance of Three Kingdoms story to ever take part in battle, Zhu Rong is one fearsome woman. Not only was she brave, she was also capable in combat and intelligent. Wife of the Barbarian King, Meng Huo, she led an army to invade Shu after her husband was cowed by Zhuge Liang's advancing troops. She did so well in battle, in fact, that she captured 2 of Shu's generals; Zhang Yi and Ma Zhong. Her next opponents were the highly decorated generals Zhao Yu, and Wei Yan. Both these generals fled without much of a fight, but Zhu Rong smelled a rat and did not give chase. She was no fool as Shu had a trap set for her indeed. Yet when Wei Yan turned back to hurl insults at her, her emotions got the better of her and she gave chase. She fell into Shu's trap and was captured. By then, she had already left a deep impression as a woman not to be messed with.
desc.content2:
Zhu Rong did not ride on a "Giant Elephant". That privilege was reserved for King Mu Lu, an ally of the Southern Barbarians. Zhu Rong is good at taming elephants and use them in war, as well as calling them back after war.

"Fiery Blade" could be referring to Zhu Rong's incredible knife throwing skills. It is said that she never misses at her throwing. Of course, it also has little to do with the ability mechanics of grabbing a card from the opponent. It is probably just paying tribute to her skill.
desc.content3: Riding out to attack Shu and capturing Zhang Yi and Ma Zhong. Significant as this was for showing her ability, this entire scene was described in only 1 paragraph in the story.

desc.content1:
The Machiavellian. The Julius Caesar of Ancient China... or at least that is what the story teller wants you to think of Cao Cao. This guy is one interesting character; ruthless, intelligent, user of ploys and ambushes, but yet chivalrous at the same time. He treats his loyal subjects and talents with respect and admiration, yet is paranoid and fearful of assassinations. Perhaps it is due to him using the "unpolitically correct" way of achieving success (through ploys, ambushes, trickery and murder) that is the cause for his negative portrayal in the story. It is said that the story itself was heavily edited to downplay Cao Cao's accomplishments so as to emphasize Liu Bei's confucius-styled leadership. Either way, there is really no denying that Cao Cao is one great leader of his time and one of the most famous Chinese historical figures of all time.

He ascended to prominence during the waning years of the Eastern Han dynasty. In his early years, Cao Cao's failed attempt to assassinate Dong Zhuo forced him to flee and wander from place to place. It was during this turbulent time that he famously proclaimed, "I would rather betray the world than let the world betray me." Subsequently, he got involved in the campaign against the Yellow Turban Rebellion and began his ascension to power. After receiving Emperor Xian and establishing Xuchang as the new capital, Cao Cao consolidated his power by bringing the central government under his direct authority. Through his victories over formidable warlords like Yuan Shao and a series of successful military campaigns, he succeeded in unifying a significant portion of northern China under his rule. Despite his defeat by the coalition forces of Sun Quan and Liu Bei at the Battle of Red Cliffs, Cao Cao's faction continued to exert influence in the north by establishing military-agricultural colonies and undertaking significant water conservancy projects. These initiatives played a crucial role in restoring and developing the social economy of the region under his control. Cao Cao was not only a skilled military strategist but also a talented poet and calligrapher. His expertise in these areas had a profound influence on later generations. After Cao Cao's death in Luoyang at the age of 66, his son Cao Pi succeeded him and granted his father the title "Emperor Wu" and the temple name "Taizu."
desc.content2: "Villainous hero/ Treachery" must be one of the best thought-of skills in the game. It is not ridiculously powerful or god-like, very useful in the game, and a wonderful fit for Cao Cao's personality in the story. Every time he met defeat, Cao Cao would bounce back and bite the face off his opponents, then convince the enemy generals to surrender into his service. Such was the case of Zhang Liao, who surrendered to Cao Cao after Lv Bu was defeated, then became one of the Kingdom of Wei's great generals. It was also mentioned that Cao Cao loves only women who have already been taken, then relish the thought of snatching the woman away from her partner.
desc.content3:
He set the foundations for the Kingdom of Wei, which became the precursor unifying the whole of China under the rule of Sima Zhao. Though by that time, Sima Zhao had usurped Wei for his own and established the Jin Dynasty of China (AD 265 - 420), nevertheless Cao Cao really was the father of all that groundwork. No Cao Cao, no Jin Dynasty, history would be completely different! Simple as that.

Cao Cao experienced endless headaches in his dying days. To cure him, his subjects brought him the most famous doctor of the time, Hua Tuo, who diagnosed his sickness as some form of rheumatism of the skull. Hua Tuo proposed to hack Cao Cao's head open for surgery, which in the century AD 200 is pretty much insane! Cao Cao, being all paranoid about assassination attempts, tossed Hua Tuo into jail for the suggestion. Without the doctor to cure him, Cao Cao succumbed to the sickness (whatever it was) soon after.

desc.content1: The incredible Hulk of ancient China. Dian Wei was reputed to have superhuman strength and was Cao Cao's trusted bodyguard. Sadly, though, most people who know the story would remember Dian Wei for his premature death. While alive, he was extremely loyal to Cao Cao and was always seen by Cao Cao's side. He uses a unique pair of Halberds that weighs 20kg each (yeah right!!!) that is never again mentioned in the story after his death. Even in death, Dian Wei still got airtime in the story because every time Cao Cao passed nearby Dian Wei's tomb, he would pay a visit and weep for the loss of his bodyguard.
desc.content2: ”Forceful Assault” is related to the circumstances that killed Dian Wei. His enemies knew that Dian Wei is feared because of his dual halberds. Just prior to the attack, a defector stole his pair of halberds while Dian Wei was sleeping. When Dian Wei awoke during the chaos, he found his halberds missing but fought on with his bare hands anyway. Dian Wei then grabbed enemy soldiers and swung them about like batons, using the enemy themselves as weapons. However, Dian Wei was still wounded and slowly bled to death, all the while fighting like a mad man and killing many before he took his last breath. Therefore the connection between "discarding a weapon card" and his halberds getting stolen. There is also less direct link between "reducing 1 unit of health" and his fight to the death.
desc.content3:
Ironically, his famed strength and his nickname "E Lai of ancient times" comes from one singular event that was not battle-related at all. It was when Cao Cao first met Dian Wei that Cao Cao asked to see what he could do, sort of like an "interview". Dian Wei saw a huge banner that was toppling in strong winds. A whole crowd of soldiers could not keep the banner upright in that wind. Dian Wei grab the banner and held it upright and steady with only one-hand. It was then that Cao Cao exclaimed "You are the E Lai of ancient times".

Cao Cao wanted to take the widow of Zhang Ji as his concubine. Zhang Ji's distant nephew, Zhang Xiu, was pissed. Knowing this, Cao Cao plotted to kill Zhang Xiu, but Zhang Xiu threw a surprise attack instead on Cao Cao. This battle is known as the battle of Wancheng and it was where Dian Wei was killed as mentioned before.

desc.content1: Long-serving bodyguard of Cao Cao, Xu Chu was said to be a huge person. His height was about 2 meters and his waist 52 inches. Probably one of the 20 most famous characters in Romance of Three Kingdoms, he has significant air-time in the story for the many instances that stood forth to protect Cao Cao. His combat ability was reputed to be on par with Zhang Fei, Guan Yu and company. His moment of glory came in the fictional account of him stripping off all his armor for his fight with Ma Chao. This bravado mixed with insanity helped him secure a place in history.
desc.content2: In his duel with Ma Chao, the two generals were fairly matched for over a hundred rounds. Xu Chu then momentarily backs off, strips off all his armor, then goes back to continue the duel bare-chested! This act of madness is followed later by his discarding his sword and grabbing Ma Chao's spear under his armpit. The ensuing struggle snaps the spear in two. The two then hack at each other with their halves of the broken spear. No wonder that Ma Chao later describes Xu Chu as a tiger gone insane.
desc.content3:
He rose to fame as a renowned general serving the Cao Wei faction during the turbulent era of the Three Kingdoms. During his youth, Xu Zhu organized the local villagers to fend off bandit attacks. Subsequently, he pledged allegiance to Cao Cao and emerged as one of his most formidable and trusted generals. Xu Zhu, renowned for his exceptional courage, earned the moniker "The Ferocious Folly" due to his fearless demeanor. His unwavering bravery and loyalty endeared him to Cao Cao, who placed immense trust in him. In the intense clash at the Battle of Tongguan, he boldly removed his armor and confronted Ma Chao in a fierce duel, both warriors evenly matched and neither emerging as a clear victor in the end. Following Cao Pi's ascension to the throne, he bestowed upon Xu Zhu the prestigious title of Marquis of Wansui Village, subsequently elevating him to the position of General of the Military Guards. Xu Zhu's unwavering loyalty and exceptional bravery were evident throughout his life, as he achieved remarkable military feats that significantly contributed to the establishment and stability of Wei.

Xu Chu probably died of old-age. He served 3 generations of Cao Cao's family: under Cao Cao, followed by Cao Pi after Cao Cao's death, followed by Cao Rui after Cao Pi's death. His loyalty to the Cao family was unquestioned. He was then posthumously titled “Marquis of Strength”.

desc.content1: It is said that Cao Cao would have won the war if Guo Jia had lived longer. In the early days of Cao Cao's expeditions, there were usually more than one advisor by Cao Cao's side, but Guo Jia's analysis of situations and predictions always seemed to be correct. It is a pity that Guo Jia and Zhuge Liang belonged to two different chronological segments, and thus never had a chance to compete head-to-head. Despite knowing his days were numbered, Guo Jia could accurately predict how the near future would unravel. On realizing that Guo Jia was absolutely right about how things played out even weeks after his death, Cao Cao wept for the loss of this amazing talent. Like Dian Wei, Guo Jia died much too early and severely handicapped the potential of Cao Cao's army.
desc.content2: Brilliant!!! "Jealousy of Heaven" translates as "heaven is jealous of a person with such immense intellect/skill/potential" and is morbidly used to describe rare talents who died young. Chinese like to think that heaven struck them down out of jealousy. Guo Jia's "Jealousy of Heaven" seems to mock the heavens as he can benefit from heaven's will (judgement cards). "Last Strategy" is equally apt as it almost re-enacts Guo Jia's contribution to Cao Cao after dying. Read more about the story behind "Last Strategy" below.
desc.content3:
After Yuan Shao and his sons Yuan Shang and Yuan Xi were defeated by Cao Cao, Guo Jia died of illness. He left Cao Cao a secret strategy that said Yuan Shang and Yuan Xi would flee to join Gongsun Kang and told Cao Cao not to pursue. Guo Jia predicted that Yuan Shang and Yuan Xi's heads would be sent by Gongsun Kang as long as Cao Cao did not make any move against Gongsun Kang. This went against the advice of many of Cao Cao's generals, who felt they should crush the brothers while they were weak. True enough, everything that Guo Jia predicted came true.

During the northern expedition to eradicate the Yuan brothers, Cao Cao's army travelled light for speed but was ill-prepared for the terrible weather. Guo Jia fell sick along the journey and was unable to join Cao Cao at the battles. By the time Cao Cao returned after the battle was won, Guo Jia had already succumbed to his illness. He was 37.

desc.content1: Best remembered for the scene where he lost his eye, Xiahou Dun is a ferocious combat general with balls of steel and fiery determination. He is one of Cao Cao's most loyal generals and is supposedly a distant relative. The story goes that Xiahou Dun was caught in an ambush and suffered an arrow to his eye. His troops were panic-stricken, thinking that their general had fallen. But then Xiahou Dun grabbed the arrow and yanked it out of his head, pulling with it his eyeball skewered on the arrow shaft. With one gulp he swallows his own eyeball, then charges right back at the assailant. Needless to say, the assailant died a horrible death when Xiahou Dun got to him. His act of swallowing his eye-ball rallied his troops' morale and scared the shit out of his enemies. Henceforth, the name of Xiahou Dun has become synonymous with bravery and gung-ho.
desc.content2: "An eye for an eye". The most straightforward link to the story is where Xiahou Dun impales Cao Xing for firing the arrow that wasted his eye. It is said that Xiahou Dun impaled Cao Xing's face before trampling his body beneath his horse. In the game, this ability is a very effective deterrent to opponents. A wonderful psychological link to the story in terms of the effect Xiahou Dun has on his enemies.
desc.content3:
In the historical account, Xia Hou Dun dies of an illness at an age of approximately 50 - 60 years old. But this defers from the Romance of Three Kingdoms story which dramatized his death as being caused by an encounter with Guan Yu's ghost. In the olden days, illnesses were very much attributed to supernatural reasons, thus it is not too far-fetched for readers to believe (especially in the 1300's when the story was written).

He played a crucial role as a general under Cao Cao during the late Eastern Han dynasty. Xiahou Dun's bravery and martial skills made him renowned at just fourteen. Xiahou Dun was one of the first generals to join Cao Cao's cause and achieved significant military victories. However, he suffered a serious injury to his left eye from an arrow during the campaign against Lv Bu. In a display of unparalleled resolve, he extracted the lodged arrow and consumed his own eyeball. This harrowing act, though seemingly paradoxical, served as a catalyst for a surge in his courage and fortitude, propelling him forward into the heart of battle. Despite dedicating most of his years to the rigors of military service, he remained steadfast in his commitment to learning and self-improvement. He would seek out his teachers in person, greeting them with respect and deference, eager to gain wisdom and guidance from their  knowledge. Xiahou Dun was known for his frugality, as he selflessly distributed all the rewards he received to his soldiers. Throughout his life, he eschewed personal possessions, leaving his family with no inheritance upon his passing. He is considered one of the founding generals of Wei.

desc.content1: Perhaps less famous than his cousin Xiahou Dun but a one of the pillars of Cao Cao's army, Xia Hou Yuan was highly praised for his efficiency in logistics. He has been credited as one of the main reasons Cao Cao rebounded from his defeat at the battle of Red Cliffs so quickly, thanks to his reliable and prompt logistics handling. In the battlefield, Xiahou Yuan earned his nickname "Cheetah of the West" for his lightning fast capture of the cities on the western border of the Wei Kingdom. Unfortunately, many of these details would have been left out if you had read the simplified version of Romance of Three Kingdoms. The big highlight of Xiahou Yuan was his death at Hanzhong under the hands of Huang Zhong.
desc.content2: Xiahou Yuan was known for his speed in moving his troops and catching his opponent’s off-guard. In his effort to capture the city of Chang Li, Xiahou Yuan discarded most of his supplies so as to be able to achieve surprise. This is probably the link behind "God Speed" option 2 ability. In general, Xiahou Yuan in the game does bring an element of speed since he can choose to ATTACK immediately even before drawing cards. Though this speed is of no consequence whatsoever in terms of dealing damage, it does make him a very useful character since he will always be able to use ATTACK on any player at least once a turn.
desc.content3:
In his attack on Song Jian, Xiahou Yuan was able to capture the city within two months, a very short time span in those days. Song Jian and all his officers were massacred by Xiahou Yuan, which ultimately scared the balls off the other opponents in the nearby region. Within a year, most of these opponents surrendered to Wei out of fear of Xiahou Yuan.

The tragedy of Xiahou Yuan is the glory of Huang Zhong from Shu. The story goes that Huang Zhong set his troops up a hill overlooking Xiahou Yuan's camp at Hanzhong. Xiahou Yuan got upset that his camp was being spied on and rallied an attack on Huang Zhong. Huang Zhong held firm till Xiahou Yuan's troops became weary from fighting uphill, then led a massive counter-attack downhill. Huang Zhong himself cut Xiahou Yuan into two.

desc.content1: Together with Guo Jia, Xun Yu was one of Cao Cao's most trusted advisors. In fact it was Xun Yu who introduced Guo Jia into Cao Cao's service, together with a host of other high-calibre individuals such as Xun You and Sima Yi. In one of the most controversial moves of the era, Cao Cao's escort of the emperor, resulting in Cao Cao wielding immense political power, was suggested by Xun Yu. However Xun Yu's heart was always for the protection of the Han Dynasty. When Cao Cao ultimately intended to abolish the Han Dynasty and establish the kingdom of Wei, Xun Yu opposed through oblique statements. This did not sit well with Cao Cao, and thus Xun Yu fell out of favor. He passed on soon after.
desc.content2: “Tiger against Wolf”, the literal translation is "rouse the tiger to eat the wolf". It is referring to Xun Yu's amazing strategy where he proposed to Cao Cao to have Liu Bei rewarded to the post of magistrate. Then Cao Cao can command Liu Bei to attack Yuan Shu. The sinister strings behind it all is Xun Yu knows the rule-abiding Liu Bei will listen to any order given by a superior, which is Cao Cao at that time. Xun Yu also knows that Yuan Shu is an egomaniac and secretly tells him that Liu Bei is coming to attack him. This way, both of Cao Cao's enemies are put at loggerheads and he benefits regardless of outcome. "Eternally Loyal" refers to Xun Yu's undying loyalty to the Han Dynasty, though its relation to the mechanics of the ability is weak.
desc.content3:
To keep the write up compact, I left out one other party other than Liu Bei and Yuan Shu, and that is Lu Bu. In summary, Xun Yu grasped the personality of all 3 of Cao Cao's enemies and pit them against each other.

Though Xun Yu is reported to have died of illness, his death followed soon after his distaste for Cao Cao abolishing the Han Dynasty. It is thus rumored that Cao Cao had Xun Yu murdered so as not to let this talent cause him problems in future.

desc.content1:
He is the beginning of the end of the Three Kingdoms era. If you have never read Romance of Three Kingdoms, we are talking about a man of ambition so great, it transcended two generations after his death before it was achieved. Portrayed as cunning and untrustworthy with promises, Sima Yi is practically demonized by the story. No doubt he was the most important military advisor the Kingdom of Wei ever had, time would never dilute his usurper legacy that erased Wei off the face of the planet. As tribute to this incredibly smart yet devious historical figure, the card game has aptly given Sima Yi abilities that make him a formidable opponent. Meet the most dangerous character in the entire game.

He played a pivotal role as a statesman and military strategist of the Wei kingdom during the Three Kingdoms period. Demonstrating exceptional intelligence and knowledge from a young age, Sima Yi caught the attention of Cao Cao, who recognized his talents and recruited him. Sima Yi was often described as having a sharp and predatory gaze that could unsettle those around him. Sima Yi held critical official positions and played a crucial role in assisting Cao Pi in securing the throne. He later became a prominent figure in Wei.
desc.content2: It is generally well-known that Sima Yi was a smart and shrewd strategist, but no, he was not smart enough to tinker with heaven's will. The abilities themselves do not reflect Sima Yi as well as how these abilities are used during game play. As a "Judgement Tinkerer", Sima Yi can just crouch inactively, waiting for judgement cards to appear before he rears his ugly head. This somewhat resembles the life mission of Sima Yi as he lay in wait for the right moment, then pouncing on opportunity to usurp an entire Wei kingdom for his own.
desc.content3:
The man that started the end of the Three Kingdoms era. Sima Yi handed down power to his son and grandson, who ultimately concurred all of China to form the Jin Dynasty (AD 265 - 420).

Sima Yi's strategic prowess was evident in his successful resistance against Zhuge Liang's Northern Expeditions and his subsequent seizure of power within the Cao Wei government during the Coup of Gaopingling.

Sima Yi died of old-age at 72. His son Sima Shi took over the reins and strengthened his family's control over Wei, followed by his grandson Sima Zhao  who united China.

desc.content1: Supposedly one of the top 5 generals of the Kingdom of Wei, Zhang Liao did not initially intend to follow under Cao Cao. In fact, Zhang Liao offered his neck for Cao Cao for beheading rather than choose to surrender. While Zhang Liao was serving Lv Bu, word had already went around of Zhang Liao's combat ability and his righteous character. Guan Yu once asked him why he was serving for a man such as Lu Bu and Zhang Liao could only look down in shame. The two later became fast friends, though circumstances dictate they had to be enemies. Zhang Liao achieved renown under Cao Cao as a steadfast and competent general. His title of "General of the Front" was given to him by Cao Pi after Cao Pi succeeded Cao Cao.
desc.content2: This part of the story occurs in the battle of Hefei where Sun Quan, ruler of Wu, led a massive army to attack Hefei. Zhang Liao was one of 3 Wei generals guarding Hefei, but they only had a combined army of 7000 men, many times less than the enemy. Leading the troops out, Zhang Liao bravely charged into the enemy and his combat prowess scared the balls off his enemy. Sun Quan's troops started deserting, not really knowing how large Zhang Liao's army was. Sun Quan climbed up a hill and saw that Zhang Liao was vastly outnumbered. He ordered his troops to encircle Zhang Liao. Unexpectedly, Zhang Liao suddenly charged out of the encirclement (thus the name "Sudden Strike") with a handful of troops and made straight for Sun Quan. The rest of Zhang Liao's troops cried out to him, "General! Have you abandoned us?!" Hearing this, Zhang Liao turned back and rescued his troops from certain death. Both parties grew weary and tired, thus they broke battle and headed back to camp.
desc.content3:
His cursing and swearing at Cao Cao and his refusal to surrender. This occurred early on in the story where Zhang Liao's leader Lv Bu had been executed by Cao Cao. In contrast to Lv Bu, who pleaded for mercy, Zhang Liao cussed Cao Cao endlessly and refused to surrender. Cao Cao was pissed off enough to bring a sword and wanted to decapitate him personally. Zhang Liao responded by sticking his neck out and welcoming death. Liu Bei and Guan Yu intervened and pleaded for Zhang Liao to be spared. Impressed by Zhang Liao's righteousness and courage, Cao Cao then put down his sword and personally unbound Zhang Liao. It was thus that Zhang Liao came to respect Cao Cao the same and, thence, began a great leader-servant relationship.

Zhang Liao suffered an arrow wound after rescuing Cao Pi from Wu forces. Though they were all eventually rescued by Xu Huang, Zhang Liao succumbed to his wound and passed on soon after.

name: Younger Zhuge Liang
title: Xu Huang
sub: Rebirth of Zhou Yafu/Rebirth of Great Warrior

desc.content1: One of the 5 most important generals to Cao Cao, Xu Huang is a man of righteousness. He first came to Cao Cao's attention as a general of Yang Feng, an enemy leader that Cao Cao intends to vanquish. Seeing Xu Huang's strong combat ability, he convinces Xu Huang to join him. Xu Huang agrees to defect, but refused the request to murder Yang Feng, exclaiming that he would not do such a dishonorable deed. Xu Huang then became fast friends with Guan Yu in the short period that Guan Yu was with Cao Cao, but when they eventually stood on opposite sides of the battle field, Xu Huang made clear to Guan Yu that his duty was kill him and cannot let personal ties get in the way. Such integrity and responsibility was highly appreciated by Cao Cao, who likened him to Zhou Yafu.
desc.content2: In the story, Xu Huang carried out raids to cut off enemy’s rations a number of times. The most notable was his raid on Yuan Shao's supplies during the battle at Guandu, which sealed Yuan Shao's defeat. Years later, Xu Huang effectively carried out a blockade during his battle at Fancheng against Guan Yu. He instructed his troops to surround the nearby city of Yancheng with trenches. The enemy troops at Yancheng, thinking that their rations have been cut off, abandoned their position and gave Xu Huang a strong foothold from which to attack Guan Yu. What the troops of Yan Cheng did not know was that the blockade was a fake, as Xu Huang's troops were too green for outright battle at that point.
desc.content3:
Xu Huang first used military tactics to cover the weakness of his army (inexperienced and awaiting reinforcement), then rode on its strengths when reinforcement came. By using diversionary tactics, Xu Huang routed Guan Yu and attacked from an unexpected direction, causing confusion within Guan Yu's camp. Amidst the confusion, a major strike was coordinated that crushed Guan Yu's main force. A broken Guan Yu then retreated to Maicheng where he met his end.

Xu Huang was shot in the head by an arrow fired by Meng Da during a battle at Xincheng. Xu Huang did not die immediately but physicians could not save him. He died later that night at age 59.

name: Liu Shan
title: Cao Ren
sub: Grand Marshall of Wei/The Big General

desc.content1: Every leader has their most loyal and trustworthy followers. Cao Ren was such a follower of Cao Cao. From Cao Cao's rise to power, till Cao Cao's death, Cao Ren was a formidable and trustworthy general in his camp that never once showed any signs of disloyalty nor surrender. In fact, Cao Ren saved Cao Cao a couple of times. Yet it must be said that Cao Ren receives a lot less mention in the story than many other of Cao Cao's generals (such Xu Chu and Dian Wei). Perhaps such is the fate of those who keep the world turning behind the scenes.
desc.content2: This is an interesting one. There actually is one scene in the story where Cao Ren was entrenched in his city, pretty much helplessly on the defensive. When Guan Yu attacked the city of Fancheng where Cao Ren was situated, Guan Yu starved the city and flooded it by redirecting river flow. Despite the odds, Cao Ren kept his troops' morale up and held out till reinforcements came. This is probably where the "Entrenched" ability arises from.
desc.content3: Right after the battle of Red Cliffs, where Cao Cao got his ass whooped bad, Zhou Yu pressed on and attacked the city of Jiangling. Cao Ren was tasked to protect Jiangling and sent his aide Niu Jin and 300 troops to defend, but these troops were losing the fight and could not escape. Cao Ren personally rode out to rescue them. His troops respect Cao Ren so much that after they exclaimed, "General, you are truly a man from Heaven!"

desc.content1:
There are those who analyze volumes of data before they can predict the future, and then there are those talents who only need to follow their gut instinct. Zhang He was one of those talents, and his predictions were sometimes more accurate than the scholarly military advisers. Widely regarded as one of the five most elite generals in the Wei Kingdom, Zhang He stood shoulder to shoulder with the likes of Xu Chu, Zhang Liao and Xu Huang just to name three. But some say Zhang He was the most outstanding amongst all five elites. His military career spanned a staggering 47 years and his military predictions outdid even the infamous Sima Yi.

For a taste of his abilities, Zhang He crushingly defeated one of Shu Kingdom's strongest generals and sent Zhuge Liang weeping as this defeat general of his was to be executed. Does this story ring a bell?
desc.content2: This ability seems to relate strongly to the word "Flexibility" instead of how it applies to Zhang He. It comes from the description “Zhang He was famed for his flexibility”, and suits the historical Zhang He in two ways. Firstly, Zhang He was able to predict his enemies’ moves, and use various tactics to defeat them. Secondly, he served multiple warlords before he joined the Wei forces, so he was “flexible” in terms of allegiance. Yet it seems that neither these are very congruent to the ability in this character card. No matter. We're still glad that his abilities make him a huge asset to any team!
desc.content3:
This ability seems to relate strongly to the word "Flexibility" instead of how it applies to Zhang He. It comes from the description “Zhang He was famed for his flexibility”, and suits the historical Zhang He in two ways. Firstly, Zhang He was able to predict his enemies’ moves, and use various tactics to defeat them. Secondly, he served multiple warlords before he joined the Wei forces, so he was “flexible” in terms of allegiance. Yet it seems that neither these are very congruent to the ability in this character card. No matter. We're still glad that his abilities make him a huge asset to any team!

Cause of death - Struck by an arrow in Mumen Trail. When Zhuge Liang retreated in his fourth expedition to the North, Zhang He pursued the Shu troops under the order of Sima Yi. Zhang He had warned Sima Yi that an ambush by the Shu forces was very likely, but Sima Yi urged Zhang He to press on. Tragically, Zhang He was right. When he entered the narrow pass in Mumen Trail, the Shu troops set off their ambush and Zhang He was struck by a crossbow to his right knee. He died from that wound, possibly due to inflammation.

desc.content1: The wife of "Conqueror of Jiang Dong" Sun Ce. Da Qiao is a very minor character in the story that has virtually zero air-time. Emerging from a distinguished family lineage and exuding unparalleled beauty, she is celebrated alongside her sister Xiaoqiao as the "Two Qiaos of Jiangdong." As the elder of the "Two Qiao sisters", she is supposedly incredibly beautiful, but a person of few words. She can play Guqin (an ancient seven-string Chinese musical instrument) and sing as well as her younger sister, Xiao Qiao, but she seldom openly performs. It is said that she only performs for her love, Sun Ce. In the story, she became part of Zhu Ge Liang's trick to ruse Zhou Yu to war with Cao Cao, though her effect on him was not as pronounced as that of Xiao Qiao.
desc.content2: There is almost no reliable link bridging these abilities to the story line. All we know is Da Qiao is beautiful, thus with "National Beauty/Captivating" she could be so beautiful perhaps that her beauty can mesmerize the enemies into forgetting about the battle?
desc.content3: During the fourth year of the Jianan era (199 AD), Sun Ce seized Wancheng and claimed Da Qiao as his concubine. Just four months later, Sun Ce was assassinated, leaving Da Qiao a widow. Despite her tragic circumstances, Da Qiao continues to be admired for her beauty and resilience.

desc.content1: The veteran of Wu Kingdom, Huang Gai served 3 members of the Sun family beginning from Sun Jian, then his eldest son Sun Ce, and finally Sun Quan. Fiercely loyal, he is best remembered for his sacrificial role in the battle of Red Cliffs. So prominent was his role, in fact, that his name has become synonymous with the "Trojan Flesh ploy" in the story.
desc.content2: A marvelous fit! Huang Gai suffered terrible pain and physical abuse, thus “Sacrificial Injury/Self Sacrifice”, all for the sake of gaining a strategic advantage (i.e.: deceiving Cao Cao). In this card game, this effect is reproduced well whereby the additional cards can make a big difference.
desc.content3: Most famous achievement - The igniter of the flames that burnt Cao Cao's fleet. The ploy is the brainchild of Zhou Yu and Huang Gai, where Zhou Yu needs Huang Gai to feign his defecting over to join Cao Cao. However Cao Cao knows that Huang Gai is very loyal, thus more extreme measures need to be used. An elaborate show was set up in the Wu whereby Huang Gai interrupts Zhou Yu and advises surrender. In a show of fury, Zhou Yu orders Huang Gai executed. The other generals and advisors all plead for Huang Gai's life and Zhou Yu finally relents, lightening the punishment from death to 50 strokes of the staff. Huang Gai's flesh was bloodied and torn after the beating, but everyone was fooled. Cao Cao's spies reported this incident and thus it became believable that Huang Gai's defection was real. When the time came for Huang Gai's defection, he sailed over to Cao Cao's fleet with small boats filled with flammable material. By the time Cao Cao realized it was a trick, it was too late. Huang Gai set the boats aflame and the fire spread throughout Cao Cao's army. Thus Cao Cao suffered the greatest defeat in his career.

desc.content1: Gan Ning, also known by his courtesy name Xingba, rose to fame as a distinguished general serving under Sun Wu during the turbulent Three Kingdoms era.
